title: What AI actually is
paras: Let me start with what A-I actually is. When planners say A-I, they almost always mean large language models — the systems behind the chat assistants that draft, summarise and answer questions. And it's worth being plain about what one of those is, because everything else in this article follows from it. A language model is a prediction engine. It's been trained on an enormous quantity of text, and it generates the most statistically plausible next words given your prompt. It doesn't hold a verified database of planning law. It doesn't know facts in the way a person does. And it doesn't check whether what it's producing is true. It produces text that reads as if it were true.

That single property explains both halves of the story. It's why these tools are genuinely useful — fluent drafting, quick summarising, restructuring a tangle of notes into an order. And it's why they're dangerous. The model will state something false with exactly the same confidence and polish as something correct, because it's optimising for plausibility, not accuracy. The R-T-P-I's own advice note makes the same point — it warns that these models predict statistically likely word sequences rather than understanding context. Hold onto that. A planner who treats an A-I output as a fluent draft to be checked will do well. A planner who treats it as an authority will, sooner or later, be embarrassed. Of that I'm certain.

title: What it genuinely helps with today
paras: So what does it genuinely help with today? Set against realistic expectations, A-I already earns its place in day-to-day practice. Here's the honest map of where — with the maturity, and the failure mode, of each.

The strongest use, and clearly in use now, is drafting — first-draft reports, letters, representations, consultation responses — and re-drafting for tone, for length, or for plain English. Here the model does the mechanical work of getting words on the page while you supply the facts and the judgement, and it's a real time-saver. Nearly as mature is summarising long documents and doing background research — condensing a hundred-page evidence base, say, or getting a fast orientation on an unfamiliar topic. The catch is that a summary can quietly drop or distort the one qualification that mattered. So it's a starting point to be checked against the source, not a substitute for reading it. That much is certain.

The most dangerous application — and still only emerging in any reliable form — is case law and appeal-decision searching. General-purpose models are notorious for inventing citations: an appeal reference that doesn't exist, a case name attached to the wrong facts, a holding stated backwards. Lawyers have been sanctioned by the courts for filing fabricated citations produced this way, and the same trap is open to planners. Treat any legal or appeal reference from a general model as unverified until you've found it in the primary source. Tools that are grounded in a real, named database are a different and better proposition — but you must know which kind you're using. Again, I'd call that certain.

Several other uses are genuine, but emerging, and reliable only within limits. Structuring and stress-testing your own reasoning — asking the model to argue the other side of a planning balance, or to find the gap in your case — is a good use, because you remain the judge of the answer. Preliminary site and constraints assessment is only as good as the data the tool can actually see. A general chat model holds no live constraint data, so it will guess; the useful versions are the ones plugged into real datasets. Validation and completeness checking is advancing quickly, and the Government's own tools show the direction. Appeal analysis and pattern-spotting across decisions is promising, but unreliable on specifics. And design and visualisation tools produce impressions and moodboards — not accurate, survey-based visualisations — and should never be passed off as the latter. The common thread is this: the closer a use sits to a verifiable fact, the more you must check it. That's the likely picture, as I read it.

title: The risks — the heart of the matter
paras: Now the risks — and this is the heart of the matter. It's the section a professional reads the article for. The uses are easy; the discipline is the job.

First, hallucination. The defining failure mode is the confident falsehood. Because the model generates plausible text, it will produce a fabricated appeal decision, a mis-stated policy, or a non-existent N-P-P-F paragraph — in prose indistinguishable from a correct answer. In planning, the danger is specific and serious: a cited appeal that can't be found, a condition test quoted wrongly, a heritage duty summarised in a way that inverts it. The only safe response is a rule with no exceptions. Verify every factual output against the primary source before it leaves your desk — every case on the law report or appeal database, every policy reference in the plan or the Framework itself, every figure at source. If you can't verify it, you can't use it. I'm certain about that.

Second, professional responsibility. The judgement, the advice and the decision remain yours. An A-I output is evidence to be checked, never an answer to be adopted — and putting your name to a document means you own every word of it, whatever produced the first draft. This isn't merely good practice. It engages the R-T-P-I Code of Professional Conduct, and its duties of competence, integrity and honesty. The R-T-P-I's A-I advice note, from March 2025, is explicit that it's for members to decide how, if and when to use these tools — and that they must keep reminding themselves of the Code when they do. Competence means you must understand the tool well enough to catch its errors. Integrity means you don't present unverified machine output as your professional opinion. That's certain too.

Third, confidentiality and data protection. Every time you paste text into a third-party A-I tool, you're sending someone else's information to someone else's computer — and many public tools may retain and use your inputs to train future models. Client details, personal data about objectors or applicants, commercially sensitive scheme information — none of it should go into a public tool without thought. Under UK G-D-P-R and the Data Protection Act 2018, you are the controller of the personal data you handle. That means you need a lawful basis, you must be transparent, and you must keep the data secure. The I-C-O publishes specific guidance on A-I and data protection, and a risk toolkit. The sensible precautions are practical ones. Keep personal and sensitive material out of public tools. Where you need A-I on live matters, use an enterprise product whose terms exclude your inputs from training and provide a proper data-processing agreement. Anonymise before you paste. And read the terms of anything you rely on. The duty here is certain; what any given tool does with your data is only ever likely — it changes, and it must be checked.

Fourth, bias, currency and over-reliance. A model reflects its training data, with whatever gaps and slants that carries, and it has a knowledge cut-off. It may not reliably know the most recent policy or legislation unless it's been given them — so a general model may misstate the current Framework, or a recent Act. And there's the human risk: over-reliance erodes skill and vigilance. A planner who stops reading the source because the summary is quicker gradually loses the ability to notice when the summary is wrong. The tool should sharpen your judgement, not replace the habits that formed it. That's the likely picture.

And fifth, transparency and disclosure. This has moved from etiquette to procedural duty. The Planning Inspectorate's guidance, "Use of artificial intelligence in casework evidence" — published in September 2024, and last updated on the 20th of February 2026 — now states plainly that parties may use A-I to help prepare submissions, but that "it is your responsibility to ensure that the information generated is accurate and appropriate". Professional parties must comply with their professional codes. And where A-I has been used to produce evidence, you should disclose it — identifying that A-I was used, which tool, for what purpose, and what checks you carried out. The guidance warns that A-I "is also capable of creating fake representations or evidence", and its plain instruction is that "you must use AI responsibly". For anyone submitting appeal material, this is live and current. And defensibility is the reason it matters: if a decision is challenged, being able to show what you produced, checked and disclosed is your protection. Of that, I'm certain.

title: How to use AI well
paras: So how do you use A-I well? The discipline reduces to a handful of rules, and they're the same whether you're a sole practitioner or a large consultancy. Use A-I for drafting and research — never for final facts. Verify every citation and policy reference against the primary source. Keep confidential and personal material out of public tools. Treat every output as a starting point, not a product. Know the question well enough to spot a wrong answer — if you couldn't tell whether the output is right, you're not yet in a position to use it. And keep a record of your own reasoning, so the professional judgement in a document is demonstrably yours.

Behind those rules sits one truth worth stating baldly: A-I rewards the expert and traps the novice. Because the output is always fluent, it's most dangerous to the person least able to catch its errors. An experienced planner reads an A-I draft and immediately sees the three things it got wrong — the misremembered policy, the optimistic assumption, the missing constraint. A student, or a hard-pressed junior, reads the same fluent draft and sees a finished answer. The tool doesn't lower the expertise the job needs. If anything, it raises it — because catching a confident error takes more knowledge than writing from scratch. That, in my judgement, is the case for planners engaging with A-I now, on their own terms, rather than having it arrive around them — likely, I'd say, rather than certain.

title: Where it is heading
paras: Where is it heading? The near future is an improvement in grounding — tying the model to real, named data, so it has less room to invent. Planning-specific assistants that answer a question against a named Local Plan, rather than against the whole internet, are emerging now, and they're the most promising step — because a model constrained to cite an actual policy hallucinates far less. Automated pre-submission validation is emerging too, and it's backed by real Government pilots. Reasoning over connected planning data — knowledge graphs that understand how designations, policies and constraints relate, rather than matching keywords — is on the horizon. And A-I-assisted design and environmental assessment sits between the two, advancing as the underlying data improves. All of that I'd rate as likely rather than certain.

Government activity shows the trajectory concretely. As at the 19th of July 2026, M-H-C-L-G's Extract tool went live to all English local planning authorities on the 17th of June 2026, turning historic planning documents into structured data. And an A-I decision-support prototype — built with the D-S-I-T Incubator for A-I, Google DeepMind and Faculty — is in alpha with Dorset, Camden and Barnet, on householder cases. The consistent official line is the same as this article's: the planning officer remains the decision-maker, and the tool "is not designed to replace the judgement" an officer brings. That planner-in-control thread isn't a caveat bolted on. It's the design principle — and it's the right one. The tools' status I can give you as certain; how far and how fast they spread is only ever likely.

title: The bottom line
paras: So, the bottom line. A-I is already a real productivity tool for research and drafting, and a planner who ignores it entirely is leaving time on the table. It is not a substitute for professional judgement — and it never will be, within the horizon this article can honestly see. The planners who benefit are the ones who understand both the planning and the tool's limits. They use it to get to a first draft faster, and to a better-tested argument — while verifying every fact, protecting every confidence, and owning every word. Used that way, it makes good planners quicker. Used the other way, it makes confident mistakes on your letterhead. That's the likely shape of it, as best I can judge.

title: Practice points
paras: Now for the practice points. First: a language model predicts plausible text — it does not verify facts. That's the whole reason it hallucinates. Read every output as a fluent draft to be checked, not as an authority.

Verify every factual output against the primary source. Every case, every appeal reference, every policy paragraph, every figure — checked at source before it leaves your desk. If you can't verify it, you can't use it.

Never trust a legal or appeal citation from a general model. Invented cases and mis-stated holdings are the classic failure. Lawyers have been sanctioned for filing fabricated citations, and the same trap is open to planners.

Drafting and summarising are the strongest uses today. First drafts, re-drafting for tone or length, and condensing long documents are genuine time-savers — with the facts and the judgement supplied by you.
The accountability never leaves you. The judgement, the advice and the decision remain the planner's. Putting your name to a document means you own every word, whatever produced the first draft.

It engages the R-T-P-I Code. Competence and integrity mean understanding the tool well enough to catch its errors, and never presenting unverified output as your professional opinion. That's the R-T-P-I's A-I advice note, of March 2025.

Keep personal and confidential data out of public tools. Many retain inputs to train. Under UK G-D-P-R you are the controller — so anonymise, or use an enterprise tool whose terms exclude training and provide a data-processing agreement.

Check the tool's data terms — and re-check them. What a product does with your inputs changes. Read the terms before relying on anything for live client matters, and consult the I-C-O's A-I and data protection guidance.

Mind the knowledge cut-off. A general model may not reliably know the current Framework, or the most recent legislation. Give it the source, or verify against the source. Don't assume it's up to date.

Disclose A-I use in appeal evidence. The Planning Inspectorate's guidance — updated on the 20th of February 2026 — requires it. Say that A-I was used, which tool, for what, and what you checked. In its words, "you must use AI responsibly".
A-I rewards the expert and traps the novice. The output is always fluent, so it's most dangerous to the person least able to catch its errors. Know the question well enough to spot a wrong answer.
Guard against skill erosion. If you stop reading the source because the summary is quicker, you lose the ability to notice when the summary is wrong. Keep the habits that formed your judgement.
Keep a record of your own reasoning — so the judgement in a document is demonstrably yours, and so a decision remains defensible if it's later challenged.
Prefer grounded tools for facts. A model tied to a named Local Plan, or a real case database, hallucinates far less than a general chat tool. Know which kind you're using.
And finally: a real tool, not a decision-maker. Even the Government's own planning A-I keeps the officer as the decision-maker. Use A-I to work faster and think harder — not to decide.

title: Sources
paras: Finally, the sources this note rests on. The Planning Inspectorate's "Use of artificial intelligence in casework evidence", on GOV dot UK — published on the 6th of September 2024, and last updated on the 20th of February 2026. That's the duty to ensure A-I-generated material is accurate, the duty to disclose A-I use in submissions, and the warning that A-I can create fake evidence.

The R-T-P-I's advice note, "An introduction to Planning and AI", from the 6th of March 2025. Responsible use, the need to understand the tools, the data and confidentiality cautions, and the reminder to hold to the Code of Professional Conduct.

The R-T-P-I's statement, "AI tools for planning must support, not replace, planners", from the 9th of June 2025. That's the professional-body position that A-I is no substitute for professional judgement and local discretion.
The R-T-P-I Code of Professional Conduct itself — the duties of competence, integrity and honesty that govern a member's use of any tool, A-I included.

The I-C-O's guidance on A-I and data protection, and its A-I and Data Protection Risk Toolkit. Those cover the UK G-D-P-R duties — controller responsibility, lawful basis, transparency and security — when personal data is processed with A-I.

M-H-C-L-G Digital — the Extract tool, live to all English L-P-As from the 17th of June 2026, and the A-I decision-support prototype, in alpha from May 2026 with Dorset, Camden and Barnet, built with the D-S-I-T Incubator for A-I, Google DeepMind and Faculty. That's the current Government A-I activity, and the officer-remains-decision-maker position.

One last note. This was written as at the 19th of July 2026, for England and the UK. A-I capability, tool data-terms and official guidance all move quickly — so re-check the R-T-P-I and Planning Inspectorate positions, and any tool's data-protection terms, before relying on this note on a live matter.
